stories.headline: Man killed in unprovoked downtown Denver assault; suspect arrested near scene
stories.summary:
A man died Saturday evening after being violently attacked in the 1300 block of Larimer Street near North Speer Boulevard in downtown Denver. A witness told 911 dispatchers that the suspect, identified as Edgardo Jimenez-Alvarad, 30, repeatedly stomped on the apparently unresponsive victim before lifting a large rock and throwing it onto the victim's head.

Officers were called to the scene around 6:30 p.m., found the victim unconscious, and transported him to the hospital, where he was later pronounced dead. Police have not released the victim's identity. A witness provided a detailed description of the suspect, and officers took Jimenez-Alvarad into custody near 14th and Champa streets within minutes of the assault.

Investigators recovered a large rock believed to have been used in the attack. According to detectives, no verbal or physical altercation preceded the assault and the victim never moved during the attack. Jimenez-Alvarad was booked into Denver County Jail on suspicion of first-degree murder.

stories.headline: Pilot killed as firefighting aircraft crashes into Colorado reservoir near Gold Mountain fire
stories.summary:
A firefighting aircraft crashed into Silver Jack Reservoir in southwestern Gunnison County on Sunday evening while engaged in operations against the Gold Mountain fire near Ouray. The body of the pilot, believed to have been the sole occupant, was recovered from the reservoir Sunday night by the Montrose County Sheriff's Office dive team.

The Gunnison Regional Communications Center received a call at 5:17 p.m. reporting the aircraft had gone down in the reservoir northeast of Ouray. The type of aircraft was not immediately identified. The pilot's next of kin had not yet been notified as of Sunday night, and no further identification was released.

The death marks the latest fatality in Colorado's severe 2026 wildfire season. Three federal firefighters — Emily Barker, 38; Nick Hutcherson, 27; and Sydney Watson, 27 — were killed on June 28 while battling the Snyder fire in western Colorado. The Gold Mountain fire has now burned 35,622 acres near Ouray and remained only 13% contained as of Sunday night.

stories.headline: Update: Aspen Acres fire reaches 35% containment at 98,100 acres; Beulah residents set to return home Monday
stories.summary:
The Aspen Acres fire, Colorado's largest active wildfire and the seventh-largest in state history, reached 35% containment as of Sunday morning, up from earlier figures, with the burn area holding at approximately 98,100 acres. Fire officials said they are feeling good about conditions near Beulah, and residents who evacuated nearly two weeks ago will tentatively be allowed to return home beginning Monday, pending any change in fire conditions.

Residents must have obtained a re-entry card at the Disaster Assistance Center at 29 Lehigh Ave. before accessing their homes, and re-entry will be staggered by address. Those living beyond 4632 North Creek Road will not yet be permitted to return. The fire has destroyed at least 851 structures, including at least 337 homes and four businesses in Pueblo and Custer counties.

Colorado's four active wildfires — Aspen Acres, Gold Mountain, Ferris and Willow — have collectively consumed an estimated 203,923 acres, or more than twice the size of Denver. The origin of the Aspen Acres fire remains under investigation, though officials believe it was human-caused.

stories.headline: New Green Ridge fire sparks evacuations south of Steamboat Springs; Gov. Polis declares emergency
stories.summary:
A new wildfire broke out Sunday in Routt County roughly 17 miles south of Steamboat Springs, near Stagecoach Reservoir, prompting Governor Jared Polis to declare a disaster emergency. The Green Ridge fire had zero containment as of Sunday night with approximately 25 acres burning, but an estimated 480 structures were under threat.

Mandatory evacuations were ordered for an area bordered by Stagecoach Reservoir to the west, Woodchuck Hill to the south, Service Creek to the east and Blacktail Mountain to the north. Pre-evacuation orders were also put in place for a broader area west of the mandatory zone. The cause of the fire was not immediately known.

The new blaze adds to an already severe wildfire season in Colorado, with four major fires already burning across the state. Officials said the situation remained rapidly developing and that updated information would be released as conditions change.

stories.headline: Near-record heat grips Denver metro; extreme heat warning in effect for parts of Colorado
stories.summary:
A powerful ridge of high pressure is driving dangerous heat across Colorado, with Denver metro area highs climbing into the upper 90s Sunday and expected to approach record territory again Monday. Denver's record high for Sunday is 102 degrees, and temperatures are forecast to stay just below that mark. Parts of western Colorado are reaching triple digits.

An Extreme Heat Warning remains in effect through Monday night for northwestern Colorado, while a Heat Advisory covers parts of southwestern Colorado. An Ozone Action Day Alert was also in effect for the Denver metro area through Sunday afternoon, with haze linked to a mix of wildfire smoke and pollutants trapped under the high-pressure ridge.

Healthofficials advise residents spending time outdoors to stay hydrated, take breaks in shade and apply sunscreen frequently. The heat pattern is expected to ease only gradually through the week, with temperatures remaining above average and fire danger elevated, particularly in the mountains. Monsoon moisture and storm chances may return by next weekend.

stories.headline: EchoStar, one of Colorado's largest companies, files for bankruptcy protection after regulatory delays block key asset sales
stories.summary:
EchoStar Corp., based in Douglas County, has filed for bankruptcy protection for its wireless and satellite television subsidiaries after regulatory delays prevented the company from receiving $4.75 billion in payments owed to noteholders. The filings were made June 30 in a Houston bankruptcy court, representing the latest chapter in a turbulent saga for the company and its Dish Wireless subsidiary.

EchoStar negotiated two major asset sales last year to avert a complete collapse — a $23 billion deal with AT&T for a portion of its wireless spectrum and a $17 billion agreement with SpaceX for another chunk. However, while the FCC approved both sales in May, pending Department of Justice and administrative approvals have delayed the payments, leaving EchoStar unable to cover a $4.75 billion debt payment that came due July 1.

The company said its Boost Mobile, Gen Mobile, Dish TV and Sling TV customer operations are shielded from the restructuring, meaning existing customer plans will remain in place. EchoStar employs more than 10,000 people. Company officials said the restructuring is intended to allow EchoStar to emerge stronger and better positioned for future opportunities.

stories.headline: Republic Services deploys AI robot arms at Denver recycling facility to boost waste diversion
stories.summary:
Republic Services has installed artificial intelligence-assisted robot arms at its north Denver sorting facility, adding automated technology to help manage 500 tons of daily mixed recycling as Colorado ramps up its waste-diversion mandates. The robotic arms, made by California-based Glacier and installed in May, pluck stray cardboard from conveyor lines at 60 to 70 hits per minute — well above the 40 to 50 a human worker achieves, and with fewer errors.

The facility processes 10,000 tons of single-stream recycling per month from Denver and surrounding suburbs, but roughly 2,500 tons currently ends up in landfills due to contamination or lack of commodity markets. Plant manager Steve Derus said the goal is to bring the residue rate down from 25% to the mid- to upper teens.

The expansion comes as Colorado's producer responsibility law takes full effect, requiring companies that produce large amounts of consumer packaging to pay fees that will fund curbside recycling programs statewide. WM, formerly Waste Management, last week also opened a $110 million sorting facility in Aurora capable of handling 175,000 tons of material annually as part of the broader statewide recycling push.

stories.headline: More than $67 million spent in Colorado's 2026 primaries, with big money often failing to determine winners
stories.summary:
An analysis by The Denver Post found that roughly $67 million was spent across Colorado's competitive primary races for statewide and federal office, with the vast majority going toward Democrat-versus-Democrat contests. More than a third of that total — nearly $25 million — flowed through super PACs and other outside groups operating with varying degrees of transparency.

Despite the massive spending, money proved to be an unreliable predictor of primary outcomes. Four of the most heavily funded candidates won their races, while four others lost. The Democratic gubernatorial primary alone accounted for more than $24.5 million in spending, with former New York City Mayor Michael Bloomberg's $5 million contribution to a super PAC backing U.S. Sen. Michael Bennet's failed bid standing out as the single largest individual investment.

Attorney General Phil Weiser ultimately won the Democratic gubernatorial nomination by more than 13 percentage points over Bennet despite being outspent by the Bloomberg-backed PAC. On the Republican side, nonprofit leader Victor Marx secured the gubernatorial nomination with roughly $3.2 million in direct and super PAC support. University of Colorado Denver political scientist Paul Teske noted that money's influence depends heavily on context and whether candidates are spending in comparable ranges.

stories.headline: Colorado wolf reintroduction program relies on range riders to protect livestock and build rural trust
stories.summary:
Eighteen range riders are patrolling Colorado's vast summer grazing lands this season as part of a state-sponsored effort to reduce conflicts between reintroduced wolves and ranchers' livestock. Fifteen are employed by Colorado Parks and Wildlife, with three others hired by the conservation nonprofit Defenders of Wildlife, including Mesa County range rider Christina Vander Berg, who monitors 50,000 acres of Western Slope terrain each summer.

The program has become a focal point of the ongoing tension surrounding wolf reintroduction, which was approved by mostly urban voters in 2023. Ranchers have reported wolves killing livestock, and the controversy has sparked death threats and security concerns at state wildlife meetings. More than 30 wolves now roam Colorado.

CPW wolf damage and conflict minimization manager Rae Nickerson said the range rider program's primary goal is to provide holistic support to the rural communities carrying the burden of reintroduction and to build trust over time. Vander Berg, who raises cattle and judges rodeo in addition to her range rider duties, said that regardless of personal views, the focus must be on collaboration now that wolves are established in the state.

stories.headline: Boater suffers life-threatening injuries in accident at Chatfield Reservoir
stories.summary:
A boating accident at Chatfield State Park sent one person to the hospital with life-threatening injuries Saturday afternoon. Colorado Parks and Wildlife officers responded to the scene near a dam on the southeast corner of Chatfield Reservoir shortly before 4:30 p.m. and found the injured adult on board the boat.

Officers transported the victim to the dock, where paramedics with South Metro Fire Rescue took over and transported the boater to the hospital. Information on the nature of the injuries and what caused the accident was not immediately available as of Sunday.

The incident is under investigation. Chatfield State Park is a popular recreational destination in the southwest Denver metro area, drawing heavy boating traffic during summer months.

stories.headline: Nuggets sign EuroLeague Defensive Player of the Year Alpha Diallo, a former Denver West prep star
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets have signed Alpha Diallo, a 29-year-old guard-forward who played high school basketball at Denver West, to a one-year, $1.4 million minimum contract, according to a league source who spoke with The Denver Post. Diallo was named Defensive Player of the Year in the EuroLeague last season while playing for AS Monaco, marking the culmination of a five-year run in European basketball.

Diallo had recently committed to Dubai Basketball on a multiyear deal, but a clause in that contract allowed his release if he signed with an NBA team by July 15. The Nuggets continue a pattern of signing players with Colorado ties, having also inked Reggie Jackson (Palmer High School), David Roddy (Colorado State) and KJ Simpson (CU) in recent years.

The signing adds another defensive-minded piece around Nikola Jokic. Denver now tentatively has 12 players under contract for 2026-27, with restricted free agency negotiations still pending for Peyton Watson and Spencer Jones. Despite his $1.4 million salary, Diallo will count $2.45 million toward the luxury tax under NBA accounting rules for veteran minimum contracts.

stories.headline: Rockies lose series finale to Giants 3-1 despite Jake McCarthy's second inside-the-park homer of the season
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies dropped the final game of their series against the San Francisco Giants 3-1 on Sunday, falling to a National League-worst 39-59 record heading into the All-Star break. Jake McCarthy led off the game with an inside-the-park home run — his second of the season — but the Rockies could not hold the lead, ultimately surrendering the decisive runs in the eighth inning on a wild throw by catcher Hunter Goodman.

McCarthy became the first player to record multiple leadoff inside-the-park home runs in a single season since Giants center fielder Edd Roush accomplished the feat in 1929. Antonio Senzatela, who entered at 9-2, took the loss after allowing the go-ahead runs in the eighth. San Francisco won three of four in the series and has now won 21 of its last 23 home games against Colorado.

The Rockies are 17-34 on the road this season and have lost four of their last five games overall. Colorado will open the second half of the season hosting Cincinnati on Friday night.

weather.notable: Nearly 9 degrees above the average high for this time of year.

joke.joke: Bowling alleys are amazing. Where else can you rent shoes, eat nachos, and throw a heavy ball at things?

topStory.headline: Sen. Lindsey Graham Dies at 71; Medical Examiner Cites Aortic Dissection
topStory.summary:
U.S. Sen. Lindsey Graham, Republican of South Carolina, died Saturday evening at age 71 following what his office described as a 'brief and sudden illness.' The District of Columbia Medical Examiner determined preliminary cause of death to be aortic dissection due to arteriosclerotic cardiovascular disease — a life-threatening tear in the main artery from the heart, caused by a condition commonly known as hardening of the arteries. Emergency dispatch records reference a call for cardiac arrest at a residence belonging to the senator.

Graham had returned earlier Saturday from a visit to Ukraine, where on Friday he met with President Volodymyr Zelenskyy. President Trump, speaking Sunday on NBC's 'Meet the Press,' said he had spoken with Graham after the senator's return and that Graham seemed fine aside from fatigue. Trump paid tribute to Graham on Truth Social, calling him 'one of the greatest people and Senators I have ever known' and 'a true American Patriot.' Zelenskyy said he was 'deeply saddened' by the news and noted Graham's 10 wartime visits to Ukraine and his sustained advocacy for the country's defense.

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and Israeli President Isaac Herzog also issued statements mourning Graham's death. Netanyahu called Graham 'one of Israel's greatest friends' and described him as a 'beloved friend,' while Herzog said he was 'shocked and heartbroken.' Graham had been a prominent voice in U.S. foreign policy circles and a consistent supporter of both Ukraine and Israel.

Graham was born July 9, 1955, in Central, South Carolina. He earned undergraduate and law degrees from the University of South Carolina, served as a military lawyer, and later represented South Carolina's 3rd Congressional District in the U.S. House before winning the Senate seat formerly held by Strom Thurmond in 2002. He was subsequently reelected three times. His death leaves a Senate vacancy in South Carolina, which will be filled according to state law governing senatorial succession.
